front: Minimum cost path in an m x n grid (right/down only)
back: Dynamic programming with tabulation computes min cost in O(mn) time. Use a full DP table (O(mn) space) or a rolling array (O(n) space). Reconstruct the path from parent pointers or by backtracking. Handle empty grids, single cell, and large costs with 64-bit integers.
hint: Remember DP grid accumulation: move only right or down to build the solution.
mnemonic: PATH: Plan, Add costs, Track path, Hold pointers

front: Kth smallest element in a BST: recursive vs Morris traversal
back: Inorder traversal yields values in ascending order. Recursive DFS with a counter uses O(h) space and O(k) time on average (O(n) worst). Morris traversal achieves O(1) space via threading, with O(k) average time (O(n) worst); it temporarily alters the tree.
hint: In BST, an in-order walk lists values from smallest to largest.
mnemonic: INORDER: Inorder traversal yields ordered results; kth element appears at the kth visited node.

front: ECS Fargate vs EKS: when to use and trade-offs
back: Use ECS Fargate for simple workloads with minimal ops. Use EKS for complex microservices needing Kubernetes features and more control. Trade-offs: Fargate favors simplicity (potentially higher cost); EKS offers flexibility and control but higher operational overhead.
hint: Fargate = managed simplicity; EKS = advanced control
mnemonic: FLEX: Fargate for Light workloads; EXtended control for Kubernetes (EKS)

front: S3 vs EBS vs EFS: performance, scale, use cases (AWS storage)
back: S3: object storage with virtually unlimited scalability; ideal for backups and distribution. EBS: high-performance block storage with strong IOPS; good for databases and boot volumes. EFS: shared, auto-scaling file system across AZs; fits content management and web serving.
hint: Map to three storage patterns—objects, blocks, and shared files across AZs.
mnemonic: OBS: Objects, Blocks, Shared

front: AWS real-time IoT analytics DB design (DynamoDB time-partitioning, Aurora, Redis cache)
back: Ingest events into DynamoDB with time-based partitioning for scalability, use Aurora for analytical queries on historical data, and cache current dashboard metrics in ElastiCache Redis for real-time updates.
hint: Think AWS trio: ingest at scale, analyze, then cache live views.
mnemonic: DAR: DynamoDB, Aurora, Redis

front: AWS storage tiering for large, versioned PSD assets
back: Active PSDs: S3 Standard for immediate access; older versions: S3 Standard-IA; long-term archives: Glacier Deep Archive. Use lifecycle rules to auto-transition by access patterns; CloudFront for low-latency global delivery.
hint: Tiered storage based on how often a file is accessed.
mnemonic: HWC: Hot=Standard, Warm=IA, Cold=Glacier

front: CQRS: Command vs Query with event-driven UI
back: Use CQRS to split writes from reads. The Command (updateShapePosition) validates and persists the new coordinates. On success, emit a ShapePositionUpdated event that the UI subscribes to, updating immediately. The UI can query the current shape with getShape when needed.
hint: Write-first, emit event to update the UI.
mnemonic: WRE: Write, Read, Emit

front: Blue-green deployment with partial monitoring during critical DB migration
back: Led a PostgreSQL migration with 70% monitoring coverage. Implemented blue-green deployment with automated rollback triggers. Achieved 99.99% uptime, no customer impact, and completed the migration 2 days ahead of schedule.
hint: Color-coded rollout and incomplete data guided the decision.
mnemonic: BLUE: Blue-green rollout; Low data; Up-time focus; Early finish.

front: Data-driven pilot approach to tech adoption
back: Presented data showing a 40% reduction in runtime errors, built a proof-of-concept to illustrate benefits, and started with a pilot to address concerns. Incremental adoption led to higher productivity and faster development cycles.
hint: Pilot first, prove value, scale up.
mnemonic: PILOT: Pilot, Incremental adoption, Listen to concerns, Observe outcomes, Test results.

front: Team conflict resolution (interpersonal)
back: Describe a time you resolved a disagreement with a teammate by scheduling a private talk, listening actively, finding common ground, and crafting a joint compromise that protects the relationship and work outcomes.
hint: Recall private talk, listening, common ground, and win-win compromise.
mnemonic: LISTEN: Listen, Identify interests, Seek common ground, Think win-win, End on good terms, Never escalate

front: Mediating conflicting team approaches
back: I facilitated a structured discussion to hear each side, mapped shared goals, and guided the group to a concrete compromise that satisfied both teammates and met project needs.
hint: Bridge the gap by uncovering shared goals.
mnemonic: MEDIATE: Meet both sides, Explore perspectives, Define goals, Identify compromises, Agree, Test, Execute.

front: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)
back: MVCC stores several historical versions of a row with timestamps. Readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers; writes create a new version. Conflicts resolve by version visibility, preventing lost updates.
hint: Readers use a snapshot to avoid blocking or overwriting concurrent updates.
mnemonic: Multiple Versions, Consistent Reads

front: Serializable isolation with row-level locks for account transfers
back: Use SERIALIZABLE isolation level, acquire explicit row-level locks on both accounts during transfers, and retry on serialization failures. This prevents lost updates while maintaining high concurrency by serializing only conflicting transactions.
hint: Serialize on conflict: lock involved rows and retry
mnemonic: SERIAL — Serialize, Explicit locks, Retry, Involve accounts, Acquire locks, Level SERIALIZABLE

front: Time-series query optimization: partitioning + composite index
back: Explain the plan to detect full-table scans with EXPLAIN, then partition by time and add a composite index on (timestamp, device_id) to prune data and speed range filters.
hint: Slice data by time to prune scans.
mnemonic: TIME-ID: Time partitions + Index on (timestamp, device_id) to prune data

front: DynamoDB: Sharded partition key strategy for a multi-region catalog
back: Use composite keys like product_id#shard to distribute items across shards. Enable adaptive capacity to scale writes during hot-sales; reads stay eventually consistent. Trade-off: higher write throughput and resilience to hotspots at the cost of slightly higher read latency and consistency overhead.
hint: Break the catalog into smaller shards to spread traffic at peak.
mnemonic: SHARD: Sharded Hash And Region Design

front: Optimizing slow PostgreSQL joins (5 tables, millions of rows)
back: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify bottlenecks, add targeted indexes on foreign keys and join columns, and consider rewriting the query (subqueries or join order) to obtain a cheaper plan.
hint: Plan first, then index the join keys, then rewrite for efficiency.
mnemonic: PIR - Plan, Index, Rewrite

front: Ansible multi-environment config with RBAC
back: Use inventory groups to separate dev/staging/prod; store env vars in group_vars; guard secrets with Ansible Vault; enforce RBAC via per-env SSH keys, sudoers rules, and per-environment Ansible user permissions with distinct Vault passwords.
hint: Clue: groups, group_vars, Vault, and per-env keys.
mnemonic: GVVP: Groups, group_vars, Vault, Permissions

front: Exponential backoff with jitter and circuit breaker in GitHub Actions
back: Use exponential backoff with jitter, a circuit breaker to stop persistent failures, and a matrix-driven retry plan. Add per-step and total workflow timeouts to ensure completion within 6 hours.
hint: Flaky API in CI? Plan smart retries that avoid endless runs.
mnemonic: BACKOFF: Backoff, Auto-matrix, Circuit breaker, Keep timeout, Finish cleanly

front: Ansible nginx role for idempotent config
back: Describe an Ansible playbook that uses an nginx role to install nginx, deploy identical config files, and manage the service across all hosts, ensuring the system converges to a single, drift-free state.
hint: Think drift-free, repeatable state across multiple hosts.
mnemonic: IDEA: Install, Deploy config, Enforce service, Aim for idempotence

front: Docker multi-stage builds for Node.js
back: Use a 2-stage Dockerfile: stage 1 builds with node:18-alpine to compile the app, stage 2 runs from node:18-alpine-slim and copies only dist/ and required runtime files. Use .dockerignore to exclude dev deps and caches so the final image stays under 200MB.
hint: Split build and runtime in stages to trim what ships.
mnemonic: STAGE - Stage, Transfer artifact, Avoid dev tools, Gate runtime size.

front: Node.js Docker multi-stage build for smaller images
back: Use two stages: build with full Node.js to compile, then copy only the built artifacts and production deps into a slim Alpine runtime image. Size drops 850MB->170MB (70-80%). Trade-offs: longer builds and Alpine caveats (fewer tools, stricter patching).
hint: Think stages: build big, run lean.
mnemonic: MSB: Make Stuff Big, then Ship Small.

front: Deployment concurrency control in GitHub Actions
back: Serialize deployments by using GitHub Actions concurrency and deployment environments. Create a concurrency group for the target environment, use queue-like sequencing (or cancel-in-progress) so only one deployment runs at a time, while still delivering fast PR feedback through lightweight checks.
hint: Imagine a single checkout line for deploys.
mnemonic: CQD: Concurrency, Queue, Deploy

front: GitHub Actions matrix for parallel tests with artifact management
back: Use a matrix (strategy.matrix) to run tests in parallel across configs. Each job uploads artifacts with retention. Use needs for dependency ordering. Enable continue-on-error for non-fatal steps and add a final always() job to fail if any needs.*.result != success and summarize results.
hint: Parallel runners, keep outputs and artifacts organized
mnemonic: PACT: Parallel matrix, Artifacts, Continue-on-error, Test-aggregation

front: React bundle optimization (lazy loading, code splitting, webpack)
back: Use React.lazy() with Suspense for route lazy loading; dynamic import() for heavy components; configure webpack splitChunks for vendor chunks; analyze with webpack-bundle-analyzer; apply prefetch/preload hints; enable service worker caching; and enable tree shaking to drop dead code.
hint: Split, load later, and trim the rest.
mnemonic: LIFT: Lazy load, Inspect bundles, Split chunks, Tree-shake

front: Flexbox: center a middle item while outer items hug edges (space-between)
back: Make the nav a flex container. Use justify-content: space-between to push the first and last items to the container ends. Center the middle item with margin: 0 auto; or by absolute centering (left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%)).
hint: Three items: fix edges, then auto-center the center item.
mnemonic: EMC — Edges Meet Center

front: Code splitting and lazy loading in React with bundle analysis
back: Implement code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense for on-demand loading. Use webpack-bundle-analyzer to prune deps and optimize imports. Move heavy components/libs to dynamic imports and apply route-based splitting; enable tree-shaking to shrink the initial bundle and boost TTI.
hint: Hint: Think lazy loading tied to routes.
mnemonic: DART: Dynamic imports, Analyze bundle, Route split, Tree shake.

front: Grid-based dashboard with dynamic sizing and virtualization
back: Explain a CSS Grid approach: dynamic sizing with CSS vars, smooth drag/move via transforms, and virtualization to render only visible widgets for 100+ items to prevent thrashing and stay fast.
hint: Clue: grid vars + transforms + virtualization keep 100+ widgets responsive.
mnemonic: GTV: Grid sizing, Transforms, Virtualization

front: Virtualized CSS Grid with dynamic columns and 60fps updates
back: Render only visible items (virtualization) in a grid; apply CSS transforms for smooth movement; batch DOM updates inside requestAnimationFrame to sustain 60fps for 10,000+ items.
hint: Remember virtualization - draw only what fits on screen.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: V-GRID: Virtualize, Grid, Render, Incremental updates, Dynamic columns

front: React code-splitting with lazy loading and Suspense
back: Analyze bundle with webpack-bundle-analyzer to identify large chunks; use React.lazy() for heavy components; wrap with Suspense and a loading fallback; convert images to WebP and prune unused dependencies.
hint: Split the bundle, load heavy parts on demand.
mnemonic: FAST: Bundle analysis, Async loading (React.lazy), Suspense, Trim unused dependencies.

front: 60fps real-time UI for 1000+ dynamic price cards
back: Use CSS containment to isolate layout/paint, apply will-change sparingly, enable GPU acceleration with transform3d, virtualize the list to render only visible cards, and batch updates with requestAnimationFrame to sync with the browser's rendering cycle.
hint: Isolate layout, render only visible items, and sync with animation frames.
mnemonic: CVTFVR: Contain, Visible render, Transform, Frame/batch, Virtualize, RAF

front: Faithfulness scoring for LLMs with RAG
back: Use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and human evaluation pipelines to quantify and minimize hallucinations in customer-service LLMs.
hint: Verify outputs against fetched evidence rather than guessing.
mnemonic: FAITH: Fidelity, Augmentation, Inference Truth, Human checks

front: Self-attention in transformers (query, key, value projections)
back: Each token is projected into query, key, and value; attention scores are computed as a scaled dot-product of Q and K, softmaxed to weights, then used to weight the Value vectors for the output.
hint: Treat tokens as queries and gather information from others via keys and values.
mnemonic: QKV: Quick, Keen, Valued — remember the order: Query, Key, Value

front: Medical AI hallucination detection against verified drug databases
back: Three-tier design: (1) real-time semantic matching to verified drug databases using embeddings to detect hallucinations; (2) cross-check with FDA databases, clinical guidelines, and literature; (3) enforce 99.9% confidence with automatic clinician handoff for uncertain cases.
hint: Start with trusted drug databases and a strict confidence gate.
mnemonic: ECTH: Embeddings; Cross-check; Threshold with handoff

front: Cascading hallucination-detection pipeline for LLMs
back: Cascading evaluation: fast similarity check, then factual verification via knowledge graph, then ensemble confidence scoring, with human review fallback if below threshold. Maintains FP/FN balance and sub-100ms latency.
hint: Start fast, verify, then escalate to human if needed.
mnemonic: FAVE: Fast check → Analyze KG → Verify with ensemble → Escalate to human

front: Code-switching multilingual tokenizer with shared vocab
back: Adopt subword tokenization with a shared EN/Chinese vocabulary, add language markers for switches, and apply a language-aware bias in attention to preserve meaning while minimizing tokens during code-switching.
hint: Think shared vocabulary, language markers, and attention bias for switches.
mnemonic: VAM: Vocab, Add markers, Modify attention

front: Kubernetes initContainer for pre-start DB migrations
back: An initContainer runs DB migrations to completion before the main container starts. It must exit with code 0 to proceed. If it fails (non-zero), the Pod won't start and will restart per the Pod's restartPolicy.
hint: Init first, then app; a failing init halts the pod.
mnemonic: IM0: Init migrations to 0 (success) before starting the app

front: Debugging ImagePullBackOff in Helm deployments
back: Verify the image exists in the registry and that imagePullSecrets (if needed) work. Do not use 'latest' in production; pin a specific semver tag (e.g., 1.2.3) in values.yaml. For production, set imagePullPolicy to IfNotPresent (explicitly) and provide registry credentials.
hint: Fixed tag beats 'latest' for predictable deployments.
mnemonic: PINE: Pin tag, Inspect registry, Never latest, Ensure secrets

front: Canary deployment with gradual traffic shifting
back: Use a canary deployment: route release to a small subset, monitor latency, error rate, and saturation, then gradually shift more traffic. If KPIs worsen or rollback threshold is hit, stop and revert. This minimizes user impact during updates.
hint: Clue: test in a small subset before a full rollout
mnemonic: Analogy: Canary in a coal mine — use a small group before a full rollout.

front: Kubernetes: Expose internal service externally with Ingress TLS (ClusterIP backend)
back: Use ClusterIP for internal service; expose externally via an Ingress that terminates TLS at the edge. This avoids NodePort/LoadBalancer costs and complexity; Ingress centralizes TLS, routing, and domain management.
hint: Edge TLS termination with a single ingress handles external domain
mnemonic: CLIP: ClusterIP, Ingress, TLS, Domain

front: Kubernetes Canary Deployment with Traffic Splitting
back: Create a new deployment with the update, split a small traffic slice to the canary via a service mesh Istio/Linkerd. Monitor metrics; ramp up traffic based on predefined success criteria until full rollout.
hint: Test the update with a small live fraction before full rollout.
mnemonic: CANARY: Compare, Assess, Narrow traffic, Anchor rollback, Ramp up, Yield data

front: Domain-adaptive CNN for weather-robust lane detection
back: Train with weather-aware batch normalization, synthetic weather augmentations (rain/snow), and a multi-task loss. Use domain adversarial training to align features across weathers. Prune for real-time speed and apply knowledge distillation to preserve accuracy under latency constraints.
hint: Bridge weather domains while keeping latency within real-time limits.
mnemonic: WARP-KD (Weather Adaptation, Regularization/BN, Pruning, Knowledge Distillation)

front: Comprehensive ML regularization with L1/L2, dropout, early stopping, and CV
back: Apply L2 weight decay 0.01 (optionally L1 for sparsity), add dropout 0.3, use early stopping with patience 5 epochs, and tune via stratified k-fold cross-validation to generalize across user data distributions.
hint: Think decay, dropout, patience, and stratified folds.
mnemonic: LDEF (L2/L1, Dropout, Early stopping, Folds)

front: Choosing logistic vs linear regression for churn
back: Use logistic regression when churn is a binary outcome (yes/no). Use linear regression for continuous targets like churn time or revenue loss. Choose by target variable type.
hint: Is the outcome a binary category or a numeric value?
mnemonic: B-C-N: Binary => Logistic, Numeric/Continuous => Linear

front: MLflow Tracking and Model Registry
back: Use MLflow Tracking to log parameters, metrics, artifacts, and model versions; register models in MLflow Model Registry for production deployment. This binds experiments to versioned models, enabling reproducibility from development to production.
hint: Think of MLflow as a single diary and library for experiments and models.
mnemonic: TRACK: Track parameters/metrics, Register models, Artifacts, Checkpoints; Keep provenance.

front: Kubeflow Pipelines with A/B testing and automated rollback
back: Orchestrate with Kubeflow Pipelines; track experiments and registry with MLflow; deploy via KFServing using Istio canaries; implement real-time metrics-based rollback triggers for fraud-detection models.
hint: Canary deployments and quick rollback based on live metrics.
mnemonic: VAR: Versioning, A/B testing, Rollback

front: PR curve threshold tuning for imbalanced classes
back: Plot a precision-recall curve to visualize precision vs recall across thresholds. With imbalance, precision for popular items drops at some thresholds. Increase the threshold for popular predictions or apply class weights to penalize false positives. Consider F1 or precision-focused metrics to pick
hint: Recall rises when you lower the positive threshold; watch popular items.
mnemonic: PRIDE: Precision, Recall, Imbalance, Decide thresholds, Elevate balance.

front: Defense-in-depth security for auth in real-time trading
back: Defense-in-depth: CSP to stop XSS; SameSite cookies + CSRF tokens; parameterized queries to prevent SQLi; IP allowlists with request validation and network segmentation to contain SSRF; plus monitoring and rate limiting to sustain real-time throughput.
hint: Layered defenses keep streaming data secure without slowing it down.
mnemonic: CRISP: CSP, Rate-limiting/Monitoring, IP allowlisting, SameSite/CSRF, Prepared statements

front: Zero-trust MFA with device-bound passkeys
back: Design uses WebAuthn device-bound passkeys with short-lived JWTs, plus rate limiting and distributed caching to sustain 100M+ daily auths at sub-100ms. Nonce-based request signing prevents replay attacks; hardware-backed keys bolster MFA.
hint: Think nonce signing and hardware-backed passkeys for speed and replay-resistance.
mnemonic: PASS - Passkeys, Auth JWTs, Sign nonce, Speed via cache

front: Zero-trust video conferencing with WebAuthn passkeys
back: Use WebAuthn passkeys for login, add device fingerprinting and behavioral biometrics, and enforce microsegmented access with real-time threat detection to sustain zero-trust posture.
hint: Continuous risk-based access for video meetings.
mnemonic: PASS: Passkeys, Analytics, Segmentation, Surveillance

front: Hybrid RSA/AES-256-GCM for PCI DSS secure payments
back: Encrypt a random AES-256-GCM key with RSA-OAEP (SHA-256, MGF1); encrypt data with AES-256-GCM using per-transaction IVs; store RSA private keys in an HSM-backed vault; rotate keys every 90 days; log all crypto ops; enforce m-of-n access controls.
hint: Remember to wrap the AES key with an asymmetric cipher before data encryption.
mnemonic: WRAP: Wrap the AES key, Rotate keys, Audit logs, Protect access

front: TLS 1.3 security gains vs TLS 1.2
back: TLS 1.3 removes insecure cipher suites, reduces the handshake from two round-trips to one, and enforces forward secrecy via Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE) key exchange, so past communications stay protected even if server keys are later compromised.
hint: Think faster handshakes and keys that don’t survive the session.
mnemonic: FIRE: Forward secrecy, Insecure ciphers removed, Reduced rounds, Ephemeral keys

front: ArgoCD Unknown Sync Status in Multi-Cluster GitOps
back: Diagnose by checking ArgoCD controller logs, verify Git repository connectivity, validate RBAC permissions, restart controllers, and implement health checks with a progressive rollout to ensure zero downtime.
hint: Start with logs, permissions, and safe rollouts.
mnemonic: LGRRP: Logs, Git connectivity, RBAC, Restart controllers, Progressive rollout.

front: SLO breach with exhausted error budget and canary rollout plan
back: When SLO is breached (99.9% at 500ms; you're at 99.7%) and the error budget is exhausted, expect a 20% traffic increase. Freeze features to stabilize, identify bottlenecks, optimize latency-critical services, and then roll out the feature via canary with monitoring and rollback readiness.
hint: Canary deployment under pressure
mnemonic: SPEED — Stabilize, Pinpoint bottlenecks, Execute canary, Deploy with monitoring

front: Emergency accelerated canary under error-budget constraints
back: Prioritize security, but keep observability. Use accelerated canary: start 10% traffic, increase by 10% every 5 minutes. Run old version in parallel, enable circuit breakers, rollback if latency or budget burn exceeds thresholds. Track burn rate in real time via Prometheus/Grafana; document for post
hint: Clue: Think gradual ramp, tight budget burn, and parallel fallback.
mnemonic: CANARY: Controlled rollout, Alerts, No blind deploy, Analyze latency, Rollback, Yield postmortem

front: Root-cause analysis with Prometheus & Grafana during 5xx errors
back: Spot the error-rate spike in Prometheus, correlate with latency trends (p95/p99) to spot patterns, then drill into Grafana logs for the implicated service to pinpoint the root cause.
hint: Follow the spike to latency patterns, then inspect logs.
mnemonic: REL triad: Rate, Latency, Logs

front: Latency spike diagnosis with Prometheus, OpenTelemetry, Grafana
back: Query Prometheus for p95 latency via histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) and 5xx rate via rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]); use OpenTelemetry to trace the slow path and correlate with Grafana dashboards (CPU, memory, DB pool) to locate bottlenecks.
hint: Start with p95 latency and 5xx rate, then trace across services.
mnemonic: PLOT: P95 latency, OpenTelemetry traces, Grafana dashboards, Tie together performance signals

front: Litmus Chaos blast radius debugging
back: Use Litmus Observability to trace how failures propagate across services; identify the blast radius; run narrowly scoped experiments (per service/namespace) with strict network policies and resource limits; contain and rollback before expanding.
hint: Trace propagation before widening chaos.
mnemonic: TRACE — Trace path, Restrict scope, Apply policies, Contain safely, Evolve gradually.

front: Go memory leak debugging with Prometheus and OpenTelemetry
back: Use Prometheus to monitor heap_alloc and gc_cycles; enable OpenTelemetry pprof; capture heap dumps during memory spikes; and correlate with Grafana dashboards to pinpoint the leaking goroutine.
hint: Heap and GC signals guide you to the leak before chasing code.
mnemonic: HEAP: Heap_alloc, Enable pprof, Acquire dumps, Ping Grafana

front: Multi-tier OpenTelemetry collector with buffering and fault tolerance
back: Adopt a multi-tier OpenTelemetry collector stack with buffering and durable queues. Route 100K+ metrics/s through scalable collectors, use load balancing and circuit breakers, batch data, and persist to disk-backed queues to prevent data loss during restarts.
hint: Layered collection with buffering and queues helps survive peak spikes.
mnemonic: MBQ — Batch, Buffer, Queue

front: Telemetry pipeline failure triage: network vs app vs data format
back: Inspect crash patterns in service logs, verify Kafka consumer offsets, test inter-service connectivity, and check recent telemetry data schema changes to identify network, app, or data-format causes.
hint: Follow the failure trail: logs, offsets, connectivity, schema.
mnemonic: LOGS: Logs → Offsets → Connectivity → Schema

front: High-severity incident response steps
back: Acknowledge the alert immediately; assess impact and scope; triage using runbooks; monitor core metrics; escalate to needed teams if SLA or expertise is required; provide regular updates; document actions and root cause for post-incident review.
hint: Act fast and marshal the right responders.
mnemonic: ASTEP: Acknowledge, Scan/Assess, Triage, Escalate, Post-incident review

front: SLI/SLO observability for microservices
back: Three pillars: metrics (Prometheus), logs (ELK), traces (Jaeger). SLIs: latency p99 <500ms, error rate <0.1%, availability >99.9%. SLOs use 30-day burn alerts, multi-tier thresholds, golden signals, and automated remediation to avoid alert fatigue.
hint: Hint: Remember the PLOT mnemonic for metrics, logs, and traces.
mnemonic: PLOT: Prometheus metrics, ELK logs, Jaeger traces

front: Capacity planning for 10x traffic growth
back: Measure current baseline performance metrics, multiply by 10 (growth factor), apply a safety buffer, and validate through load testing; configure autoscaling thresholds to ensure capacity meets peak demand.
hint: Multiply the baseline metrics and stress-test before launch.
mnemonic: FAST: Factor growth, Assess metrics, Stress-test, Threshold autoscale

front: Event-driven CQRS with WebSocket real-time price updates
back: Adopt an event-driven design with CQRS: separate write/read stores, idempotent endpoints, and WebSocket streams for real-time prices. Enforce rate limits and retry policies to sustain correctness at ~100k trades/day.
hint: Split reads/writes and stream real-time updates
mnemonic: CEWS: Consistency via Event streams, WebSocket updates

front: Multi-tier edge caching with CDN, regional Redis, and pub/sub invalidation
back: Design uses CDN edge caches for global reach, regional Redis clusters for low-latency reads, and a write-through invalidation path via pub/sub to keep caches coherent across locations, delivering sub-50ms responses.
hint: Edge caches first, then regional caches, with pub/sub invalidation.
mnemonic: ERIP — Edge, Redis, Invalidation Pub/Sub

front: Multi-layer load balancing for zero-downtime Black Friday traffic
back: Implement a multi-layer stack: DNS round-robin for geo-distribution, L7 proxies for intelligent routing, continuous health checks, autoscaling with metrics, circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures, and rolling updates to avoid downtime.
hint: Think globally, route smartly, and keep services alive.
mnemonic: GLHAC — Geo, L7 proxies, Health checks, Auto-scaling, Circuit breakers

front: Saga Pattern (Choreography) with Compensation
back: Choreography-based Saga with compensating actions. Use event sourcing to recover state, and idempotent compensators with retry policies and circuit breakers to handle mid-transaction market-data feed failures.
hint: Let events drive undo, not a single orchestrator.
mnemonic: SAGA = Stateful Actions; Guarded Aborts

front: Distributed rate limiting: token bucket with Redis and sliding window
back: Illustrate a Redis-backed token bucket for distributed state with a sliding-window check for precise 10k rps. Refill tokens at a steady rate, cap bursts, and apply exponential backoff on retries to deter abuse while keeping legitimate users served.
hint: Token-based guardrail across distributed requests
mnemonic: TOKEN: Token bucket, Exponential backoff, Sliding window, Open requests, Never-block legitimate users

front: ID-based horizontal sharding + multi-region leader-follower replication
back: Explanation: Use horizontal sharding with userID-based partitioning to split 10M users. Enable multi-region replication with a leader-follower setup - one primary region for writes and read replicas in others to balance load, cut latency, and sustain high uptime.
hint: Slice users by ID and read from replicas across regions.
mnemonic: ID-MR: ID-based partitioning + Multi-Region replication

front: TDD red-green-refactor cycle
back: In TDD you write a failing test for the target behavior (red), then implement the code to pass (green), and finally refactor the implementation and tests while keeping all tests green.
hint: Three-step loop: fail a test, make it pass, then clean up.
mnemonic: RGR → Run test, Get result, Refactor

front: Mutation testing
back: Mutation testing injects small, deliberate changes (mutants) into code to verify tests actually detect defects, not just run code. It complements code coverage: coverage shows what runs, while mutation testing reveals whether tests would fail if behavior changed.
hint: Tiny code tweaks reveal whether tests catch real defects.
mnemonic: MUTATE — Mutants Under Test Assess Test Effectiveness

front: Mocking internal function in Jest (jest.fn/spyOn)
back: Use jest.fn() to replace the target function with a mock, or use jest.spyOn(object, 'fn') to monitor an existing method. Run the unit under test and assert on callCount/calls to verify frequency and arguments.
hint: Watch the helper by replacing or spying on it, then count calls and inspect args.
mnemonic: MOCK IT — Mock the function, Observe calls, Check arguments, Test behavior.

front: TDD for real-time conflict resolution in collaborative editing (OT)
back: Use TDD by simulating two users editing the same element concurrently, asserting that the OT merge yields a consistent, data-preserving state. Then implement the conflict-resolution logic until all tests pass.
hint: Remember the merge algorithm that reconciles concurrent edits in real-time.
mnemonic: OT-MERGE: Overlap Tests Merge Edits; Resolve & Guarantee Consistency.

front: Jest unit test for REST endpoint: verify 200 and name field
back: Mock the HTTP request, then assert the response status is 200 and the body includes a name property. The key assertion is that name exists in the returned structure.
hint: Remember to mock and validate the response shape, not the data.
mnemonic: NAME: Name Appears; Mock, Assert, Ensure.

front: Testing strategy for distributed streaming with ordering and exactly-once
back: Unit tests for core logic; integration tests for broker coordination; chaos engineering to validate fault tolerance; mutation testing to improve test quality. Ensures message ordering, exactly-once semantics, and cluster resilience.
hint: Four-layer approach: unit, integration, chaos, and mutation.
mnemonic: UICM — Unit, Integration, Chaos, Mutation
